one_line_takeaway: Current LLM fact-unlearning methods only scrub the target fact itself and leave it reconstructable from retained, logically-connected facts; the paper formalizes this 'deep unlearning' gap, provides two benchmarks and three metrics, and shows no method exceeds 0.8 deep-unlearning success while retaining 80% accuracy.
key_finding: No evaluated method achieves Success-DU above 0.8 while keeping retain-accuracy above 0.8, on either benchmark and across all six LLMs. On MQuAKE (the easier setting, minimal set size fixed at 2), Success-DU@Acc>=0.8 stays in the 0.1-0.3 range for all four methods on both LLMs. On Eval-DU (multi-step, minimal set size up to 17) some configurations collapse to near zero (e.g. GPT2-XL: GA 0.03, NPO 0.00, TV 0.01, WHP 0.00). Deep unlearning is far harder than superficial unlearning: e.g. GA on GPT2-XL reaches Success-SU 0.80 but Success-DU only 0.03 (gap 0.77); the superficial-vs-deep gap can reach ~0.8 on Eval-DU versus at most ~0.3 on MQuAKE. Even in the white-box setting where the true minimal set is provided, gains are limited and an effectiveness upper bound persists, which the authors attribute to the inherent difficulty of large-batch unlearning. NPO preserves general capability (MMLU) far better than it preserves in-KB unrelated facts, reaching Success-DU@MMLU>=0.95FT = 1.00 on both MQuAKE models.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'deep unlearning', a new setting for fact unlearning in LLMs where success requires not only removing a target fact but also making it non-deducible from any facts the model retains via a set of logical rules. Formally, a target fact k is deeply unlearned iff k is not in the deductive closure of the retained knowledge base (K minus the removed set) under rule set R. The authors define the notion of a 'minimal deep unlearning set' (the smallest set of facts that must be removed so the target is no longer derivable, which is non-unique), propose three evaluation metrics (Success-DU, Recall, Accuracy), give a randomized approximation algorithm (Algorithms 1-3, with a correctness proof in Theorem 1) to enumerate multiple minimal deep unlearning sets since exact recall computation is NP-hard, and build two benchmarks. Using these, they empirically show that four representative unlearning methods largely fail at deep unlearning. This is the paper's OWN contribution; prior fact-unlearning metrics (TOFU, WMDP, MUSE, Patil et al., etc.) discussed in Related Work only measure whether the target fact itself is still retained, and the concurrent multi-hop unlearning work of Choi et al. is explicitly distinguished as operating in the opposite direction (propagating deletion to downstream implications rather than removing supporting facts that reconstruct the target).
threat_model: An adversary who, after unlearning, has query access to the LLM's remaining (retained) facts and can apply any logical rule in a known rule set R to deduce new facts. The privacy failure occurs when the exact target fact was directly removed (superficial unlearning succeeds) but the adversary can still reconstruct it by extracting related retained facts and combining them under the rules. The motivating example: the target '(Camila Flores, child, Wyatt Ross)' can be re-derived from retained facts '(Wyatt Ross, father, Xavier Ross)' and '(Camila Flores, husband, Xavier Ross)'. Motivation is privacy/compliance (GDPR right-to-be-forgotten, removing memorized personal relationships, work histories, addresses). Lower Recall means more residual fact combinations remain, making reconstruction easier for the adversary.
limitations: (1) The framework models knowledge and adversarial reasoning as triplets plus explicit logical rules; natural-language or probabilistic/neural deduction is left to future work, so it may under- or over-estimate a real LLM adversary. (2) Recall/Accuracy rely on an approximation algorithm because finding the exact best-overlapping minimal deep unlearning set is NP-hard; the minimal-set collection is sampled, not exhaustive. (3) Eval-DU is semi-synthetic (100 fictitious people, 400 family relations, 300 biographical facts, 48 rules) and requires fine-tuning models on it, which differs from naturally pretrained knowledge; conversely MQuAKE offers only partial observability of the model's true knowledge, which can make unlearning look more successful than it is. (4) The paper diagnoses failure and proposes no new deep-unlearning algorithm; the white-box results suggest even oracle knowledge of minimal sets is insufficient. (5) Rule sets are assumed known and correct.

datasets_used: MQuAKE (real-world multi-hop facts): 82 two-hop and 42 three-hop instances forming the knowledge base; 50 super-relation targets selected (25 two-hop + 25 three-hop); minimal deep unlearning set size always 2

Eval-DU (newly constructed semi-synthetic): 100 fictitious people, 400 family relationships + 300 biographical facts, 11 relation types and 3 biography attributes (birthyear, birthplace, job), 48 logical rules; 55 target facts (5 per relationship); minimal deep unlearning set size ranges 1-17
quantitative_results: No method reaches Success-DU@Acc>=0.8 above 0.8 while keeping accuracy >=0.8 (central negative result)
MQuAKE Success-DU@Acc>=0.8, GPT-J-6B: GA 0.15, NPO 0.11, TV 0.16, WHP 0.12; Vicuna-7B: GA 0.28, NPO 0.16, TV 0.18, WHP 0.21
MQuAKE Recall@Acc>=0.8, GPT-J-6B: GA 0.30, NPO 0.28, TV 0.32, WHP 0.36; Vicuna-7B: GA 0.48, NPO 0.37, TV 0.42, WHP 0.46

Eval-DU Success-DU@Acc>=0.8, GPT2-XL: GA 0.03, NPO 0.00, TV 0.01, WHP 0.00; Phi-1.5: GA 0.48, NPO 0.08, TV 0.25, WHP 0.02; Llama2-7b: GA 0.00, NPO 0.63, TV 0.10, WHP 0.02; Llama3-8b: GA 0.24, NPO 0.41, TV 0.31, WHP 0.06
Superficial vs deep gap: GA on GPT2-XL Success-SU 0.80 vs Success-DU 0.03 (delta 0.77); Eval-DU gap can reach ~0.8 vs MQuAKE gap at most ~0.3 (e.g. Vicuna-7B TV delta +0.43)
Success-DU@MMLU>=0.95FT: NPO = 1.00 on both GPT-J-6B and Vicuna-7B (general capability easier to preserve than in-KB unrelated facts)
White-box (oracle minimal set) Success-DU@Acc>=0.8 improves but plateaus, e.g. GPT2-XL NPO 0.00 -> 0.20, Vicuna-7B TV 0.18 gain; NPO on Llama2-7b stays 0.63 (little change)
All six LLMs achieve 1.0 accuracy on the in-scope facts before unlearning (Table III); pretrained/finetuned MMLU e.g. Llama3-8b 0.50, Vicuna-7B 0.49, Phi-1.5 0.40
Algorithm 1 finds 6-17 distinct minimal deep unlearning sets for more than half of Eval-DU facts
